steps.title: Identify what follows the blank
steps.content: The sentence reads: "his use of graffiti-like ______ often incorporate words or phrases from poetry and mythology." The word "that" begins a relative clause ("that often incorporate...") which must attach directly to the noun it modifies ("scribbles") with no intervening punctuation. Any punctuation mark between the noun and the "that"-clause breaks the grammatical connection.

steps.title: Eliminate the wrong choices and confirm B
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'scribbles : that': A colon here would signal a list or elaboration following an independent clause — "his use of graffiti-like scribbles" is not an independent clause, so a colon is incorrect. ✗
  • Choice B — 'scribbles that': No punctuation between "scribbles" and "that" — the relative clause attaches directly to the noun it modifies, exactly as Standard English requires. ✓
  • Choice C — 'scribbles ; that': A semicolon joins two independent clauses; neither "his use of graffiti-like scribbles" nor "that often incorporate words or phrases" is an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice D — 'scribbles. That': A period ends the sentence and capitalizes "That," making the relative clause a fragment with no main verb of its own. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
